SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims (attention-based transfer methods and two attention map definitions) are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence across multiple datasets and architectures, and activation-based transfer outperforming FitNets is demonstrated. The mathematical content consists mainly of attention-map definitions and L2 losses which are straightforward and appear correct. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines (KD, FitNets, double backpropagation) and diverse evaluations, though noted limitations (gradient-based transfer only applied to a small BN-free network, unclear handling of BN in second backprop, and limited hyperparameter tuning/resources for some ImageNet runs) restrict generality. Overall the conclusions are supported by the presented results, with only minor concerns about applicability scope and some implementation details.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from method (definition of activation- and gradient-based attention maps) to experiments across multiple datasets and a concise conclusion; the main contributions are stated early (attention transfer mechanism and two attention map types). Notation and definitions for the two attention mechanisms are presented clearly enough to reproduce the approach, and experiments/tables seem informative (multiple baselines, hyperparameters reported, code released). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough, though some presentation gaps remain (e.g., handling of batch normalization during the second backpropagation and limited clarity around why gradient-based transfer was restricted to one architecture), which keep it from being exceptional.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces a clearly new mechanism for teacher→student transfer by using spatial attention maps (both activation-based and gradient-based) rather than full intermediate activations, and empirically shows activation-based attention outperforms FitNets-style full-activation transfer and can be combined with knowledge distillation. This formulation is a non-trivial extension of prior distillation/hint methods (e.g., KD, FitNets) and is distinguished from related work by the specific attention-map definitions and matching losses. The gradient-based matching (input gradients) and the broad empirical evaluation across ImageNet, COCO and fine-grained datasets further differentiate the contribution, though practical limitations (second-backprop complexity, BN handling) temper its immediacy.

main_claims: Propose attention as a mechanism for transferring knowledge from a teacher network to a student network.
Define and use two types of spatial attention maps for transfer: activation-based and gradient-based attention.
Show experimentally that attention transfer yields significant improvements across multiple datasets and architectures (residual and non-residual).
Demonstrate that activation-based attention transfer outperforms full-activation (FitNets-style) transfer and can be combined with knowledge distillation.
_summary.methodology: Define spatial attention maps from CNNs (activation-based via channel-wise statistics and gradient-based via input gradients) and train student networks to match teacher attention maps using l2 losses on l2-normalized attention maps (activation-based) or on input gradients (gradient-based), optionally combined with knowledge distillation loss.
datasets: face recognition dataset referenced (Parkhi et al.)
baselines: baseline student networks without attention transfer
double backpropagation / l2 gradient norm regularizer (Drucker & LeCun)
limitations: Small input spatial resolution (e.g., CIFAR 32×32) limits the room for attention transfer.
Gradient-based attention transfer was applied only to Network-in-Network without batch normalization due to complexity of automatic differentiation and second backpropagation.
Knowledge distillation (KD) struggled or hurt convergence for some teacher–student pairs on ImageNet and when teacher and student differ in architecture/depth.
Unclear how to handle batch normalization during the second backpropagation step required for gradient-based attention transfer.
Limited hyperparameter tuning and computational resources for some ImageNet experiments.
_summary.topic_area: Computer Vision - attention transfer / knowledge distillation for convolutional neural networks
